subtitle: Two Friends, One Gallows, and a Nation’s Awakening

panels.shot: epic split-panel wide, faces filling opposite halves, faint outline of a steam train and a gallows behind them
panels.action: Ashfaq & Bismil (Kakori Comrades) stand back-to-back, solemn and unbowed, a ghostly train and a looming gallows rising behind them in a misty sepia landscape of 1920s Hindustan.

dialogue.text: In the restless years after Kakori, two friends—Ashfaqulla Khan and Ram Prasad Bismil—walked from the smoke of a train to the silence of the gallows, carrying one shared promise of freedom.

i18n.hi: काकोरी के बाद के बेचैन सालों में, अशफाकुल्ला खान और राम प्रसाद बिस्मिल धुएँ से फांसीगाह तक चले, आज़ादी का एक ही वादा उठाए।

panels.shot: wide overhead view of north Indian plains with railway line cutting through, small train in distance
panels.action: Ashfaq & Bismil (Kakori Comrades) are tiny figures on a distant embankment near a railway line under a dusty sky, the flat fields of the United Provinces stretching to the horizon.

dialogue.text: August 1925. The railway sliced through the plains of the United Provinces, carrying coin and control for the Raj, and a quiet anger for the young dreamers of the Hindustan Republican Association.

i18n.hi: अगस्त 1925। रेल उपनिवेशी प्रांतों की धरती चीरती चली, राज के लिए सिक्के और काबू, और एच.आर.ए. के नौजवान ख़्वाबियों के लिए चुपचाप गुस्सा ढोती।

panels.shot: medium two-shot at dusk, both in simple clothes, heads close, planning
panels.action: Ashfaq & Bismil (Kakori Comrades) stand under a dim lantern in a modest room, poring over a rough map on the floor, their faces lit with resolve and comradeship.

dialogue.text: The Hindustan Republican Association sought funds not for greed, but for an armed rising. In meetings by lamp, Bismil the poet and Ashfaqulla the engineer’s son became more than co-conspirators—they became brothers in purpose.

i18n.hi: हिंदुस्तान रिपब्लिकन एसोसिएशन को धन लालच के लिए नहीं, हथियारबंद बगावत के लिए चाहिए था। दीये की रोशनी में बैठकों में, कवि बिस्मिल और इंजीनियर के बेटे अशफाकुल्ला हमसफ़र से बढ़कर, मक़सद के भाई बन गए।

panels.shot: moody interior prison corridor, camera low, showing two separate cell doors with numbers, guards distant
panels.action: Ashfaq & Bismil (Kakori Comrades) are seen in separate barred cells down a narrow colonial prison corridor, each sitting on a rough cot, the air heavy with heat and silence.

dialogue.text: The train at Kakori was stopped. Currency was seized. The British intelligence struck back. Arrests swept through the HRA. Soon, the friends who had moved together were locked in separate cells, awaiting a colonial trial.

i18n.hi: काकोरी में ट्रेन रोकी गई। करंसी ज़ब्त हुई। ब्रिटिश इंटेलिजेंस ने पलटवार किया। गिरफ़्तारियाँ एच.आर.ए. पर टूट पड़ीं। जो दोस्त साथ चलते थे, अब अलग-अलग कोठरियों में बंद, फिरंगी मुक़दमे के इंतज़ार में थे।

panels.shot: close-up through bars on Bismil writing by faint light, Ashfaq faintly visible in another cell across corridor
panels.action: Ashfaq & Bismil (Kakori Comrades) appear as Bismil leans over paper on his knee, writing with intense focus, while a blurred Ashfaqulla across the corridor watches with a faint, proud smile.

dialogue.text: In Lucknow Jail, ink became their last weapon. Bismil began his Atmakatha, turning his life into a testimony. Ashfaq filled his jail notebooks and letters with steady, dignified reflections for his family and comrades.

i18n.hi: लखनऊ जेल में स्याही उनका आख़िरी हथियार बनी। बिस्मिल ने आत्मकथा लिखनी शुरू की, ज़िंदगी को गवाही में बदलते हुए। अशफाक ने जेल की कॉपियाँ और खत अपने घरवालों और साथियों के लिए ठहरी हुई, ख़ुद्दार बातों से भर दीं।

panels.shot: wide shot of colonial courtroom, judge high on bench, accused dock visible, crowd of lawyers and onlookers
panels.action: Ashfaq & Bismil (Kakori Comrades) stand together in the prisoners’ dock of the Chief Court of Oudh, wearing simple jail clothes, facing a stern bench of British and Indian judges in a tall, echoing courtroom.

dialogue.text: The Kakori Conspiracy Case came before the Chief Court of Oudh at Lucknow. The charge: waging war against the King-Emperor. The court became a stage where youth, law, and empire stared each other down.

i18n.hi: काकोरी साज़िश केस लखनऊ में औध चीफ कोर्ट के सामने आया। इल्ज़ाम: बादशाह-ए-हिंद के ख़िलाफ़ जंग छेड़ना। अदालत एक मंच बन गई, जहाँ जवानी, कानून और साम्राज्य आमने–सामने टिक गए।

panels.shot: medium shot on Bismil in dock, slight low angle, shoulders straight, lawyers blurred behind
panels.action: Ashfaq & Bismil (Kakori Comrades) are seen with Bismil at the front of the dock, chin raised, hands resting calmly on the rail, as lawyers argue animatedly in the background.

dialogue.speaker: ashfaqulla_khan_and_ram_prasad_bismil
dialogue.text: If love for my country is a crime, I accept your verdict.

i18n.hi: अगर वतन से मोहब्बत जुर्म है, तो मैं तुम्हारा फ़ैसला मंज़ूर करता हूँ।

panels.shot: medium shot on Ashfaqulla giving statement, frontal, eyes clear, slight smile
panels.action: Ashfaq & Bismil (Kakori Comrades) appear with Ashfaqulla stepping slightly forward in the dock, his face calm and open, as he addresses the bench with folded hands.

dialogue.text: Court records preserved their voices. Bismil spoke of duty, of a future republic. Ashfaqulla, the first Muslim hanged in a political case after 1857, declared with quiet pride that he had chosen his path with open eyes.

i18n.hi: अदालती रेकॉर्ड ने उनकी आवाज़ें सहेज लीं। बिस्मिल ने फ़र्ज़ और आने वाली गणतंत्र की बात की। 1857 के बाद पहले मुसलमान सियासी मुजरिम के तौर पर फाँसी पर चढ़ने वाले अशफाकुल्ला ने ठंडी शान से कहा कि उन्होंने रास्ता खुली आँखों से चुना है।

panels.shot: close-up on their hands gripping the dock rail side by side, slight low angle
panels.action: Ashfaq & Bismil (Kakori Comrades) stand shoulder to shoulder at the dock rail, their hands gripping the worn wood inches apart, veins taut yet composed.

dialogue.text: The court saw ‘accused’. The nation, reading newspapers and pamphlets, began to see something else—two friends, Hindu and Muslim, holding one line together against the might of the Raj.

i18n.hi: अदालत को ‘मुजरिम’ दिखे। मगर अख़बारों और पैम्फ़लेट पढ़ती कौम को कुछ और दिखा—दो दोस्त, एक हिंदू, एक मुसलमान, जो राज की ताक़त के सामने एक ही सफ़ में खड़े थे।

panels.shot: over-the-shoulder from judge’s bench looking down at dock as judgment is read
panels.action: Ashfaq & Bismil (Kakori Comrades) stand motionless as the judge, high above, reads from a stack of papers, the packed gallery straining to hear.

dialogue.text: In 1927, the judgment fell. The Chief Court confirmed death sentences for Ram Prasad Bismil, Ashfaqulla Khan, and two others. Legal language tried to weigh rebellion; the noose was the final punctuation.

i18n.hi: 1927 में फ़ैसला गिर गया। चीफ कोर्ट ने राम प्रसाद बिस्मिल, अशफाकुल्ला खान और दो और साथियों की फाँसी बरक़रार रखी। क़ानूनी भाषा ने बगावत तौलने की कोशिश की; आख़िरी नुक्ता फाँसी का फंदा बना।

panels.shot: medium shot in prison yard, the two close together under a tree, guards distant, evening light
panels.action: Ashfaq & Bismil (Kakori Comrades) walk slowly side by side in a walled prison yard, chains light on their ankles, their faces turned toward each other in a quiet, intense exchange.

dialogue.speaker: ashfaqulla_khan_and_ram_prasad_bismil
dialogue.text: We began together, dost… we will end together.

i18n.hi: हमने साथ सफ़र शुरू किया था, दोस्त… साथ ही मुकाम पर उतरेंगे।

panels.shot: intimate interior, close on Bismil writing a poem, faint image of a gallows reflected in his inkwell
panels.action: Ashfaq & Bismil (Kakori Comrades) appear as Bismil writes a poem on rough paper, his eyes shining, while in the shadowed background Ashfaqulla prays quietly on a mat.

dialogue.text: Awaiting death, their pens did not rest. Bismil wrote verses of surrender only to God and motherland. Ashfaqulla wrote to his family, asking them not to weep, but to be proud of his choice.

i18n.hi: मौत की आहट पास थी, पर उनकी कलमें नहीं रुकीं। बिस्मिल ने ऐसी कविताएँ लिखीं जो सर झुकाती थीं बस ख़ुदा और मातृभूमि के आगे। अशफाकुल्ला ने घर वालों को लिखा—रोना मत, मेरे फ़ैसले पर फख्र करना।

panels.shot: close-up split frame, half on Bismil’s face in prayer, half on Ashfaqulla’s face in prayer, both eyes closed
panels.action: Ashfaq & Bismil (Kakori Comrades) are shown in a quiet double close-up, each in his own cell, hands raised in different traditions of prayer, faces peaceful and firm.

dialogue.text: Different faiths shaped their last rituals, but the center of their prayer was the same. They offered their lives as nazr and ahuti, sacrifice, to the dream of a free Hindustan.

i18n.hi: रूढ़ियाँ अलग थीं, पर प्रार्थना का मरकज़ एक था। उन्होंने अपनी जानें एक साथ नज़र और आहुति बना कर, आज़ाद हिंदुस्तान के ख़्वाब पर चढ़ा दीं।

panels.shot: evocative corridor shot, camera behind them as they walk separately toward light, ropes unseen
panels.action: Ashfaq & Bismil (Kakori Comrades) are led from opposite ends of a dim corridor by warders toward bright doorways, their silhouettes straight, the rough stone walls closing in around them.

dialogue.speaker: ashfaqulla_khan_and_ram_prasad_bismil
dialogue.text: Our bodies will fall; our promise must walk on.

i18n.hi: हमारी देह गिरेगी; हमारा वादा चलता रहना चाहिए।

panels.shot: symbolic wide shot of village square with people reading newspapers, portraits of martyrs hung on a wall
panels.action: A small town bazaar in the United Provinces buzzes softly as men and women gather around a newspaper reader, while hand-drawn portraits of Bismil and Ashfaqulla hang garlanded on a plastered wall.

dialogue.text: When the hangings were carried out in December 1927, the British records closed the file. But in towns and villages, poems, pamphlets and stories opened a new chapter, naming them Shaheed, martyrs for a shared land.

i18n.hi: दिसंबर 1927 की फाँसियों के बाद ब्रिटिश रेकॉर्ड ने फाइल बंद कर दी। पर कस्बों और गाँवों में कविताएँ, पैम्फ़लेट और किस्से खुल गए, उन्हें शहीद कहकर, साझा सरज़मीन के लिए न्यौछावर मानते हुए।

panels.shot: montage wide, statues, schoolbooks, and a modern railway platform all seen in one sweeping composition
panels.action: A sweeping view shows a school mural of Bismil and Ashfaqulla, a small town statue garlanded on an anniversary, and a modern Indian railway platform where a train stands under the name Kakori.

dialogue.text: From court judgments and jail letters, their story traveled into songs, textbooks and memorials. Later generations learned that a Hindu poet and a Muslim patriot had walked the same path, hand in hand, to the gallows.

i18n.hi: अदालती फ़ैसलों और जेल से निकले ख़तों से उनकी कहानी गीतों, किताबों और यादगारी स्मारकों तक पहुँची। आने वाली पीढ़ियों ने जाना कि एक हिंदू शायर और एक मुसलमान सूरमा हाथों में हाथ डाले, एक ही राह से फाँसी तक गए।

panels.shot: heroic low-angle wide, spectral figures of both standing together over a map of India, sunrise behind
panels.action: Ashfaq & Bismil (Kakori Comrades) appear as luminous, larger-than-life figures standing side by side over an outline map of India, a soft dawn rising behind them, their expressions gentle yet unyielding.

dialogue.text: In every age of division, their friendship returns as a quiet command: let creed and background bow before a just cause. Remember Kakori, remember their promise, and let unity be the sharpest weapon of freedom.

i18n.hi: हर फ़साद के दौर में उनकी दोस्ती धीमी आज्ञा बनकर लौटती है: मज़हब और नस्ल को पाक मक़सद के आगे झुकने दो। काकोरी को याद रखो, उनके वादे को याद रखो, और एकता को आज़ादी का सबसे पैना हथियार बनाओ।

subtitle_i18n.en: Two Friends, One Gallows, and a Nation’s Awakening
subtitle_i18n.hi: दो दोस्त, एक फांसी, और जागती हुई कौम
